natural_language: How many distinct roles appear in the cast credits?

Correct answer is COUNT(DISTINCT role) = 357,355. Agent must know role = character name. Strata column description should clarify.

natural_language: How many movies are in the database?

Without semantic context, COUNT(*) FROM movies (211K) is the obvious query. Strata model description should clarify the kind enum.

natural_language: How many entries in the movies table belong to a series?
generated_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM movies WHERE series_id IS NOT NULL;
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM movies WHERE series_id IS NOT NULL
notes: movies has two self-referential FKs: parent_id (episode→season, season→series) and series_id (direct link to root series). 'Belong to a series' maps to series_id (135,791 entries). parent_id gives 151,096 (includes seasons whose parent is also a series). kind='series' gives 4,305 (misinterpret as 'how many ARE series'). All three produce valid-looking results. Strata column descriptions should distinguish parent_id vs series_id semantics.

natural_language: What are the different types of cross-references between movies?

Both return exactly 5 rows — row count won't reveal the error. Strata model descriptions should clarify movie_references stores inter-movie relationships.

natural_language: How many movies have been tagged with at least one keyword?
generated_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT movie_id) FROM movie_keywords;
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT movie_id) FROM movie_keywords
notes: movie_keywords and movie_categories have IDENTICAL structure: (movie_id bigint, category_id bigint) with FKs to movies and categories. Both 'tag' movies via the categories table. movie_keywords = descriptive terms (50,511 movies), movie_categories = genre/source/class/standing labels (71,083 movies). 'Tagged with a keyword' maps to movie_keywords, but an agent seeing two structurally identical junction tables may pick movie_categories. Strata descriptions should differentiate the two.

natural_language: How many movies are categorized under the Drama genre?
generated_sql:
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT mc.movie_id)
FROM movie_categories mc
JOIN categories c ON mc.category_id = c.id
WHERE c.name = 'Drama';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT mc.movie_id) FROM movie_categories mc JOIN categories c ON mc.category_id = c.id WHERE c.name = 'Drama'
notes: Requires movie_categories JOIN categories on category_id = id. Agent must infer the join key. Wrong join on root_id or parent_id produces incorrect results rather than an error.

natural_language: How many movies are categorized under the Drama genre?
generated_sql:
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT mc.movie_id) AS drama_movie_count
FROM movie_categories mc
JOIN categories c ON mc.category_id = c.id
WHERE c.name = 'Drama';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT mc.movie_id) FROM movie_categories mc JOIN categories c ON mc.category_id = c.id WHERE c.name = 'Drama'
notes: Requires movie_categories JOIN categories on category_id = id. Agent must infer the join key. Wrong join on root_id or parent_id produces incorrect results rather than an error.

natural_language: How many English-language abstracts exist for movies released before the year 2000?

SELECT COUNT(*) 
FROM movie_abstracts_en 
JOIN movies ON movie_abstracts_en.movie_id = movies.id 
WHERE movies.date < '2000-01-01';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM movie_abstracts_en a JOIN movies m ON a.movie_id = m.id WHERE m.date < '2000-01-01'
notes: Requires movie_abstracts_en JOIN movies on movie_id = id. Date filter forces the join — abstract table has no date column. Agent must select the correct abstract table (en, not de/es/fr).

natural_language: What are the 10 most commonly assigned keywords for movies?
generated_sql:
SELECT c.name, COUNT(mk.movie_id) AS assignment_count
FROM movie_keywords mk
JOIN categories c ON mk.category_id = c.id
GROUP BY c.id, c.name
ORDER BY assignment_count DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT c.name, COUNT(*) as cnt FROM movie_keywords mk JOIN categories c ON mk.category_id = c.id GROUP BY c.name ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 10
notes: Requires movie_keywords JOIN categories on category_id = id. Keywords are stored in the categories tree under root_id=8 (Keyword). Agent might confuse movie_keywords with movie_categories (genres) since both reference the categories table.

notes: Ambiguity: people table contains people entities, but image association is tracked in image_ids by object_type/object_id. Correct interpretation counts person image records in image_ids.

natural_language: How many people have image records?
generated_sql:
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT p.id) AS people_with_image_records
FROM people p
JOIN people_links pl ON p.id = pl.person_id
WHERE pl.source = 'imdbperson';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM image_ids WHERE object_type = 'Person'
notes: Ambiguity: people table contains people entities, but image association is tracked in image_ids by object_type/object_id. Correct interpretation counts person image records in image_ids.

natural_language: How many English movie titles are stored?
generated_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM movies WHERE name IS NOT NULL;
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM movie_aliases_iso WHERE language = 'en'
notes: Ambiguity: movies.name can be interpreted as title text, but language-scoped title variants are in movie_aliases_iso. 'English titles' maps to aliases with language='en'.

natural_language: How many English movie titles are stored?

notes: Ambiguity: movies.name can be interpreted as title text, but language-scoped title variants are in movie_aliases_iso. 'English titles' maps to aliases with language='en'.

natural_language: How many cast credits are attached to episode entries?
generated_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM casts c JOIN movies m ON c.movie_id = m.id WHERE m.kind = 'episode';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM casts c JOIN movies m ON c.movie_id = m.id WHERE m.kind = 'episode'
notes: One-hop join path: casts.movie_id -> movies.id. Requires semantic interpretation of episode membership via movies.kind enum, not table naming alone.

natural_language: How many cast credits are attached to episode entries?
generated_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) 
FROM casts c
JOIN movies m ON c.movie_id = m.id
WHERE m.kind = 'episode';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM casts c JOIN movies m ON c.movie_id = m.id WHERE m.kind = 'episode'
notes: One-hop join path: casts.movie_id -> movies.id. Requires semantic interpretation of episode membership via movies.kind enum, not table naming alone.

natural_language: How many cast credits are for directors with a non-empty character field?
generated_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) 
FROM casts 
WHERE job_id = (SELECT id FROM jobs WHERE name = 'Director') 
  AND role IS NOT NULL 
  AND role != '';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM casts c JOIN jobs j ON c.job_id = j.id WHERE j.name = 'Director' AND c.role != ''
notes: One-hop join path: casts.job_id -> jobs.id. Requires semantic context that Director is in jobs.name and that casts.role is character text, often empty for non-acting credits.

natural_language: How many cast credits are for directors with a non-empty character field?
generated_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) 
FROM casts c
JOIN jobs j ON c.job_id = j.id
WHERE j.name = 'Director' 
  AND c.role IS NOT NULL 
  AND c.role != '';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM casts c JOIN jobs j ON c.job_id = j.id WHERE j.name = 'Director' AND c.role != ''
notes: One-hop join path: casts.job_id -> jobs.id. Requires semantic context that Director is in jobs.name and that casts.role is character text, often empty for non-acting credits.

natural_language: What are the 10 most assigned genres across movies?
generated_sql:
SELECT c.name, COUNT(mc.movie_id) AS assignment_count
FROM categories c
JOIN movie_categories mc ON c.id = mc.category_id
GROUP BY c.id, c.name
ORDER BY assignment_count DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT c.name, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM movie_categories mc JOIN categories c ON mc.category_id = c.id WHERE c.root_id = 1 AND c.id != 1 GROUP BY c.name ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 10
notes: One-hop join path: movie_categories.category_id -> categories.id. Genre filtering requires semantic knowledge of category hierarchy where root_id=1 denotes Genre.

natural_language: What are the 10 most assigned genres across movies?
generated_sql:
SELECT 
    c.name AS genre_name,
    COUNT(mc.movie_id) AS assignment_count
FROM 
    movie_categories mc
JOIN 
    categories c ON mc.category_id = c.id
GROUP BY 
    c.id, c.name
ORDER BY 
    assignment_count DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT c.name, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM movie_categories mc JOIN categories c ON mc.category_id = c.id WHERE c.root_id = 1 AND c.id != 1 GROUP BY c.name ORDER BY cnt DESC LIMIT 10
notes: One-hop join path: movie_categories.category_id -> categories.id. Genre filtering requires semantic knowledge of category hierarchy where root_id=1 denotes Genre.

natural_language: How many title aliases belong to entries that are actual movies?
generated_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) 
FROM movie_aliases_iso ma
JOIN movies m ON ma.movie_id = m.id 
WHERE m.kind = 'movie';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM movie_aliases_iso a JOIN movies m ON a.movie_id = m.id WHERE m.kind = 'movie'
notes: One-hop join path: movie_aliases_iso.movie_id -> movies.id. Requires semantic disambiguation of movies.kind values to isolate true movies from episodes/seasons/series.

natural_language: How many title aliases belong to entries that are actual movies?

JOIN movies m ON mai.movie_id = m.id
WHERE m.kind = 'movie';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM movie_aliases_iso a JOIN movies m ON a.movie_id = m.id WHERE m.kind = 'movie'
notes: One-hop join path: movie_aliases_iso.movie_id -> movies.id. Requires semantic disambiguation of movies.kind values to isolate true movies from episodes/seasons/series.

natural_language: How many keyword assignments are attached to movies released from 2010 onward?
generated_sql:
SELECT COUNT(*) 
FROM movie_keywords mk 
JOIN movies m ON mk.movie_id = m.id 
WHERE m.date >= '2010-01-01';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM movie_keywords mk JOIN movies m ON mk.movie_id = m.id WHERE m.date >= '2010-01-01'
notes: One-hop join path: movie_keywords.movie_id -> movies.id. Release date filter lives only on movies, forcing join and requiring semantic selection of movie_keywords (not movie_categories).

WHERE m.date >= '2010-01-01';

natural_language: What are the 10 lowest-rated movies by average vote score?
generated_sql:
SELECT name, vote_average
FROM movies
WHERE vote_average IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY vote_average ASC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT name, vote_average FROM movies WHERE kind = 'movie' AND vote_average IS NOT NULL AND vote_average > 0 ORDER BY vote_average ASC LIMIT 10
notes: Uses vote_average as the quality metric and inverts direction (lowest rather than highest). Semantic layer still needs to differentiate score (vote_average) from volume (votes_count).

natural_language: What are the 10 lowest-rated movies by average vote score?
generated_sql:
SELECT id, name, vote_average
FROM movies
WHERE vote_average IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY vote_average ASC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT name, vote_average FROM movies WHERE kind = 'movie' AND vote_average IS NOT NULL AND vote_average > 0 ORDER BY vote_average ASC LIMIT 10
notes: Uses vote_average as the quality metric and inverts direction (lowest rather than highest). Semantic layer still needs to differentiate score (vote_average) from volume (votes_count).

natural_language: What are the 10 movies with the highest number of votes?
generated_sql:
SELECT name, votes_count
FROM movies
ORDER BY votes_count DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT name, votes_count FROM movies WHERE kind = 'movie' AND votes_count IS NOT NULL AND votes_count > 0 ORDER BY votes_count DESC LIMIT 10
notes: 'Highest number of votes' maps to votes_count, not popularity or vote_average. Tests metric intent disambiguation on same table.

natural_language: What are the 10 movies with the highest number of votes?
generated_sql:
SELECT name, votes_count
FROM movies
ORDER BY votes_count DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT name, votes_count FROM movies WHERE kind = 'movie' AND votes_count IS NOT NULL AND votes_count > 0 ORDER BY votes_count DESC LIMIT 10
notes: 'Highest number of votes' maps to votes_count, not popularity or vote_average. Tests metric intent disambiguation on same table.

natural_language: Among frequently voted movies, which 10 have the best average rating?
generated_sql:
SELECT name, vote_average, votes_count
FROM movies
WHERE votes_count > (SELECT AVG(votes_count) FROM movies)
ORDER BY vote_average DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT name, vote_average FROM movies WHERE kind = 'movie' AND vote_average IS NOT NULL AND vote_average > 0 AND votes_count >= 50 ORDER BY vote_average DESC LIMIT 10
notes: Requires combining quality metric (vote_average) with support threshold (votes_count >= 50). Without semantic cues, models often optimize the wrong metric.

natural_language: Among frequently voted movies, which 10 have the best average rating?
generated_sql:
SELECT 
    m.name,
    m.vote_average,
    m.votes_count
FROM movies m
WHERE m.votes_count > 0
ORDER BY m.vote_average DESC, m.votes_count DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT name, vote_average FROM movies WHERE kind = 'movie' AND vote_average IS NOT NULL AND vote_average > 0 AND votes_count >= 50 ORDER BY vote_average DESC LIMIT 10
notes: Requires combining quality metric (vote_average) with support threshold (votes_count >= 50). Without semantic cues, models often optimize the wrong metric.

natural_language: What are the 10 movies with the largest gross margin (revenue minus budget)?
generated_sql:
SELECT name, (revenue - budget) AS gross_margin
FROM movies
WHERE revenue IS NOT NULL AND budget IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY gross_margin DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT name, (revenue - budget) AS gross_margin FROM movies WHERE kind = 'movie' AND revenue IS NOT NULL AND budget IS NOT NULL AND revenue > 0 AND budget > 0 ORDER BY gross_margin DESC LIMIT 10
notes: Business metric composition: gross margin is derived from revenue and budget. Semantic descriptions must distinguish cost and earnings measures.

natural_language: What are the 10 movies with the largest gross margin (revenue minus budget)?
generated_sql:
SELECT 
    name,
    revenue - budget AS gross_margin
FROM movies
WHERE revenue IS NOT NULL 
    AND budget IS NOT NULL
    AND revenue - budget > 0
ORDER BY gross_margin DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT name, (revenue - budget) AS gross_margin FROM movies WHERE kind = 'movie' AND revenue IS NOT NULL AND budget IS NOT NULL AND revenue > 0 AND budget > 0 ORDER BY gross_margin DESC LIMIT 10
notes: Business metric composition: gross margin is derived from revenue and budget. Semantic descriptions must distinguish cost and earnings measures.

natural_language: Which 10 pre-1980 movies have the longest runtime?
generated_sql:
SELECT name, runtime, date
FROM movies
WHERE date < '1980-01-01' AND runtime IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY runtime DESC
LIMIT 10;
reference_sql: SELECT name, runtime FROM movies WHERE kind = 'movie' AND runtime IS NOT NULL AND runtime > 0 AND date < '1980-01-01' ORDER BY runtime DESC LIMIT 10
notes: Runtime ranking with temporal constraint uses movies.runtime and movies.date. Semantic layer helps avoid similarly named runtime-like fields in non-movie contexts.

notes: Runtime ranking with temporal constraint uses movies.runtime and movies.date. Semantic layer helps avoid similarly named runtime-like fields in non-movie contexts.

natural_language: How many non-binary people are recorded in the database?
generated_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM people WHERE gender NOT IN (1, 2);
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM people WHERE gender = 2
notes: gender is integer-coded (0/1/2), so text filters like gender='non-binary' fail. Semantic context must expose categorical encoding.

natural_language: How many people have unknown gender in the data?
generated_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM people WHERE gender IS NULL;
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM people WHERE gender IS NULL
notes: Unknown gender is NULL, not a numeric code. Naive filters like gender = 0 or empty-string checks are incorrect.

natural_language: How many image records use the lowercase movie object type token?
generated_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM image_ids WHERE LOWER(object_type) = 'movie';
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM image_ids WHERE object_type = 'movie'
notes: object_type values are case-sensitive; canonical movie token is 'Movie'. This deliberately tests case-sensitive categorical assumptions.

natural_language: How many genre-category nodes exist including the root genre node?
generated_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM categories;
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM categories WHERE root_id = 1
notes: Hierarchical categories include root and descendants. Questions that do not explicitly exclude root differ from the common 'leaf genre count' interpretation.

natural_language: How many cast credits are tagged as screenplay work?
generated_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM casts WHERE job_id IN (SELECT id FROM jobs WHERE name ILIKE '%screenplay%');
reference_sql: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM casts c JOIN jobs j ON c.job_id = j.id WHERE j.name = 'Drehbuch'
notes: Screenplay is represented by German canonical job label 'Drehbuch', not English synonyms. Without semantic mapping, models miss the intended job identifier.
